questions.question: What is the sum of the probabilities of all possible outcomes in a probability distribution?
questions.explanation: The sum of the probabilities of all possible outcomes in a probability distribution is always 1, or 100%. This is a fundamental property of probability theory.

questions.question: A discrete random variable can take on the values of an infinite set of possible outcomes. Which of the following types of probability distributions is an example of this?
questions.explanation: An infinite set of possible outcomes is characteristic of a Poisson distribution, which is often used to model the number of events occurring in a fixed interval of time or space.

questions.question: What is the probability that at least one of two independent events will occur?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) 1 - P(A) * P(B), which represents the probability that at least one of two independent events will occur. This can be calculated using the formula 1 minus the probability that neither event will occur.

questions.question: A random variable X has a probability distribution with the following probabilities: P(X=0) = 0.3, P(X=1) = 0.4, P(X=2) = 0.3. Which of the following is the expected value of X?
questions.explanation: The expected value of a discrete random variable can be calculated using the formula E(X) = ∑xP(X=x). In this case, E(X) = 0*0.3 + 1*0.4 + 2*0.3 = 1

questions.question: A continuous random variable X has a probability density function f(x) = 2x. Which of the following is the mean of X?
questions.explanation: The expected value of a continuous random variable can be calculated using the formula E(X) = ∫xf(x)dx. In this case, E(X) = ∫x*2x dx from 0 to ∞, which can be calculated as 1/2 using integration
